SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main technical claim that a Soft-KB lookup enables end-to-end training and better policies (Section 4) and provides a probabilistic derivation for the KB posterior (Appendix A / eq. 3). Mathematical derivations shown (e.g., posterior decomposition and derivation for Pr(Gj = i)) are plausible and internally consistent. However the method relies on strong assumptions explicitly stated in Section 3.2 (independent column values, uniform prior over rows, no new entities at test time), and the evaluation largely uses a simulated user (Section 6.2), which limits the strength of empirical validation of some claims. Overall the technical approach is sound but depends on assumptions that should temper the generality of the conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper's main novel idea is replacing symbolic (Hard-KB) queries with a differentiable Soft-KB posterior to enable end-to-end reinforcement learning (abstract and Section 4), which is a clear methodological contribution beyond prior modular API-call approaches cited (e.g., Wen et al., Williams and Zweig). The work integrates this Soft-KB with neural belief trackers and RL-based policy learning in a unified framework, which is a non-trivial extension of existing modular systems. While the idea builds on several prior components (GRU trackers, REINFORCE, entropy-based policies), the soft retrieval perspective and treating the KB posterior as a learnable/samplable component is meaningfully novel.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (method, belief trackers, policy, experiments) and most notation (Ti,j, Vj, ptj, qtj) is defined (Section 3.2 and 3.3). However the provided text contains duplicated paragraphs, some formatting/placeholder notes (e.g., [TODO:add details]) and repeated sentences which reduce readability. Figures/tables are referenced (e.g., Table 1, Figure 1) and some appendices are used for derivations and hyperparameters, but the excerpt lacks clear presentation of empirical results in-line, making parts harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental setup explores several agent variants (hand-crafted vs neural trackers and policies, No-KB/Hard-KB/Soft-KB, and an E2E agent) and multiple KB sizes (Table 1), and includes derivations and hyperparameter details in appendices (Sections 6.1, A, E). Nevertheless, the provided text does not include the main quantitative results, ablation analyses, or statistical evaluations in the excerpt, and although variants are compared, detailed sensitivity studies and real-user evaluation details are not present here. Reproducibility is helped by some hyperparameter and simulator descriptions, but more experimental detail and failure/limitation discussion would strengthen the substance.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem for goal-oriented dialogue agents—making KB access differentiable to enable end-to-end learning—which if effective could influence how dialogue systems integrate databases (abstract, Section 4). The approach opens practical directions for jointly training LU and policy and for personalized agents as claimed in the abstract. While promising and likely to be adopted or extended by researchers in dialogue and information-access systems, the impact depends on empirical robustness beyond simulated users.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—end-to-end RL for KB-backed dialogue agents—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues and targets the right audience (Sections 1, 4). The methods (neural belief trackers, GRUs, REINFORCE, simulator-based evaluation) and the evaluation protocol (comparisons of variants, multiple KB sizes) align with standards expected at such venues. The paper is positioned relative to relevant prior work cited throughout (e.g., Wen et al., Williams and Zweig).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper explicitly compares several variants (No-KB, Hard-KB, Soft-KB) and both rule-based and RL agents (Section 6.1), and situates itself relative to prior approaches that use hard symbolic queries or API calls (multiple places in related work). However, in the provided excerpt full empirical comparisons, numerical results, and statistical significance analyses are not shown, and it is unclear whether all strong recent baselines were included or tuned identically. The experimental design appears focused on fair internal comparisons but the excerpt lacks complete comparative detail.
